title: Natalie Tsutsumi Final Paper
text:
Author: Natalie Tsutsumi
Professor: Professor Roberts
Course: Cinema After Cinema
Date: 1 May 2026



With a rich history tracing back to ancient Mesopotamia (Scharcz), the creation of perfume has evolved over thousands of years and expanded rapidly within the past two centuries. As the potential for profitability within this market increased, alongside with the overall technological advancements in film technology, the marketing campaigns of perfumes shifted towards commercial films, which grew to have a distinct set of conventions. Even with the absence of smell in any of these commercials– the main selling point of a perfume– they have managed to effectively entice viewers to buy their products, with a global market size of 53.04 billion USD in 2025 (Fortune Business Insights). American and European companies dominate among the overall top perfume brands (Fortune Business Insights), with established luxury brands crafting campaigns appealing to multisensory experiences and aspirational lifestyles. Within South Korea, a rising market for the perfume industry, Korean fragrance brands have emerged with original appeals to the “story behind the scent” (Santos) and an “emotional connection”, emphasizing fragrance’s ties to personal memories (Choi). Perfume, an album released by K-pop group NCT DOJAEJUNG in April 2023, explored these ties through its fragrance-centered creative concept, taking influences from both domestic and global perfume industries. This paper will analyze how Perfume, specifically through the music video of its titular single “Perfume”, its album video teasers, and its physical album packaging, brought together many of the most compelling marketing and stylistic techniques of the perfume and K-Pop industries. Fusions of commercial and artistic values take place all throughout the music industry, yet Perfume serves as a uniquely concentrated example of this delicate balance, effectively adapting the personal connection aspects of the perfume industry to the highly fan connection-reliant K-Pop medium. This hybridization further blurs the already unclear boundary between the two values, filtering the aesthetic of perfume consumption into an emotional experience.



The origins of the K-Pop industry can be traced back to the 1990s, with the 1992 debut of hip-hop trio Seo Taji and Boys commonly acknowledged as the “definitive genesis of K-Pop” (Oak). SM Entertainment, one of the largest and most influential K-Pop agencies, is credited for introducing the “trainee system” (in which potential idols train for years before being selected to debut in a group) to the K-Pop industry after its founding in 1989 (Kim). Soo-man Lee, the founder and former executive producer of SM Entertainment, described his system for artistic development and global expansion as “cultural technology.” In a 2011 address at Stanford Business School, he explained that he saw culture as a “type of technology” that is “much more exquisite and complex” than the “information technology” of the nineties. The guidelines created at SM Entertainment for adapting “cultural technology” to targeted countries were precisely narrowed down to specifics, including optimal chord progressions, eyeshadow shades, camera angles in music videos, and hand gestures (Seabrook).
Lee continued to set out to expand upon his vision of “cultural technology.” In January 2016, he held a press conference where he announced the debut of SM Entertainment’s latest idol group. NCT, which stands for Neo Culture Technology, would have an “limitless” number of members, as it would be a group consisting of multiple subunits based in cities all around the world. (Jackson) These aspirations mostly failed to materialize, as all of the debuted subunits (NCT U, NCT 127, NCT Dream, NCT DOJAEJUNG, NCT JNJM, NCT Wish) apart from one (WayV) are based in South Korea; additionally, the end of the “limitless” member concept was marked with the 2023 debut of NCT Wish, the planned “final” unit of NCT (Benjamin). Nevertheless, throughout the past decade, each unit of NCT has merged a unique blend of global influences with Korean culture, creating a highly successful brand that has pushed its “neo” creative concepts forward.
NCT DOJAEJUNG, a subunit of NCT, debuted in April 2023 with the mini-album Perfume. The three members (Doyoung, Jaehyun, and Jungwoo) had already debuted within NCT, all being members of the NCT 127 unit. Following the official announcement of the group’s debut on March 22, 2023 (Benjamin), up until the album’s release, the group unveiled a set of photo and video teasers, a standard practice within the K-Pop industry. On April 17, 2023, the group released the music video for the titular single “Perfume,” along with the rest of the album tracks. The album sold over 672,300 copies in its first week, “setting a new record [in South Korea] for the most number of albums sold in the first week for a unit after the album’s release.” (Kim)
Within South Korea, fragrance has been utilized for thousands of years, with their use in folk rituals for spiritual purification dating back to 500 B.C. (Klove) Moving towards the present, Korean perfume brands have been rising amongst the larger Korean beauty industry, finding their place through unique scents and intentional connections between scent and memories. While previous generations preferred more “clean” and “fresh” scents, such as florals and citruses, (Santos) new brands are emerging, appealing to younger generations with bold scents tied to Korean culture. One example is BORNTOSTANDOUT, a brand founded in 2022 that offers unconventional scent notes in its unisex fragrances, like steamed rice and overripe banana. (“K-Perfume”). Another popular Korean brand, Tamburins, (endorsed by K-Pop idols Jennie from BLACKPINK and Felix from Stray Kids) combines striking packaging and elaborate architectural installations with standout scents. One of its popular fragrances, “Pumkini,” combines the scents of white pumpkin, perilla leaves, and blood oranges (Scipioni). Other brands lean more towards storytelling aspects, deliberately crafting scents that evoke specific memories. Nonfiction, a brand launched in 2019, designs scents around feelings or memories, aiming to “capture mood and atmosphere rather than [follow] conventional Western fragrance structures.” (“K-Perfume”) Vicky Jung, founder of the Seoul-based perfume collection Deacoutre, perfectly encapsulates this approach towards perfume in a written interview with The Korea Herald, stating, “Fragrance is a special language of preserving memories, of anchoring emotions in time…We remember scents more deeply than we remember faces or words. That’s why perfume matters — it lingers, even when everything else fades.” (Choi) With an estimated 2025 market size of $754.71 million (“South Korea Perfume”), the Korean perfume industry is only expected to grow, with these innovative and thoughtful approaches pushing it forward domestically and internationally and influencing the creation of NCT DOJAEJUNG’s Perfume.



While the music video for “Perfume” takes inspiration from a wide range of aspects of perfume commercials, two of the clearest areas of influence can be seen in its use of aspirational imagery and its reworking of gender conventions. Displays of aspirational lifestyles are a key aspect of Western perfume commercials. These displays frequently fall into two main categories: fantasies of luxurious lifestyles, and fantasies of natural lifestyles. The “luxury” themed commercials often feature extravagant imagery and settings, including castles with chandeliers, opulent parties, swimming pools, car drives, and sprawling cityscapes. In “The False Obvious: Gender Myth in Perfume Commercials,” a 2024 academic article written by Den Lin and Shi Huina, they note that these visuals serve to “demonstrate the dignity and superiority of the sophisticated and dreamy Western middle class.” Images of “luxury” also typically contain Western ideals of racial ideals. Lin and Huina also note that within the 45 randomly selected perfume commercials they studied, 95% contained white actors, a key aspect of “producing the ‘conservative Englishness look.’” (qtd. by Lin and Huina) This aligns with their observed tendency for Western perfume commercials to be “less localized” than other categories of commercials. (Lin and Huina). While still conforming to these ideals, “natural” themed perfume commercials appeal to consumers through aspirations of reconnecting with “desirable” aspects of nature through their fragrance. These commercials frequently use floral imagery, a key aspect of this approach. Notable examples include commercials for Gucci’s “Bloom” and “Flora” fragrances, which all contain floral imagery conveying associations with freshness and simplicity– but with an elevated twist. Most “natural” themed perfume commercials contain elements of aspirational luxury to some degree, and many combine them with more explicitly luxurious elements, such as yachts, to elements of nature, like ocean destinations.
The “Perfume” music video by NCT DOJAEJUNG contains elements of both of these categories, with an approach leaning more towards the “nature” themed aspirational displays. Many similar shots to nature-centric perfume commercials can be seen in “Perfume,” several of which are displayed below:



Figure — collage from the “Perfume” music video


These familiar conventions provided “Perfume” with an established framework to adapt, allowing it to convey a “refreshing” image– a valued trait within the K-Pop industry. Themes of aspirational luxury also play a role in “Perfume,” yet with a slightly different approach from Western perfume commercials. The music video was entirely recorded in Thailand, a choice in setting departing from the typical European destinations. Many of the scenes take place inside a rustic-style indoor dome, which aligns more with a “nature” centric image; however, other scenes take place outside a luxurious, European-style mansion, which comes complete with a swimming pool that the members dive into– showcasing themes of released inhibitions, freedom, and youth. Furthermore, NCT DOJAEJUNG, the protagonists of the “Perfume” music video, are all Asian, breaking in tradition with the typically white actor seen in perfume commercials. However, the music video also (briefly) features a female model, who is the receiver of the perfumes designed by the members. This model, shown in the collage above, is white, visually matching the common protagonist of a Western perfume commercial. The combination of these choices help distinguish “Perfume” as a product within an Asian industry, while also keeping it in alignment with a Western industry it sets out to emulate.
Western fragrance commercials frequently exhibit a gendered set of conventions, both towards men and women. Within male-marketed commercials, an attractive male model or celebrity tends to be featured, who is often shirtless or in minimal clothing that reveals a chiseled, muscular form. Other commercials showcase men wearing outfits commonly deemed as “masculine,” such as black leather jackets, white dress shirts, and navy suits. The contents of many of these commercials can be categorized into two categories: those showing the men participating in strenuous physical activities, giving the cologne an association with masculine power and strength; and those showing the men being desired by women and engaging in sexuality with them, giving the cologne an association with desirability and sexual dominance. Male-marketed fragrance commercials also typically refrain from floral imagery and the use of bright colors, especially shades of purple and pink.
“Perfume”, however, subverts many of these conventions, framing its male subjects in a “softer” manner more typical of a female-marketed perfume commercial. None of the members display muscular forms or wear any of the aforementioned typically “masculine” outfits; only member Doyoung dons a cotton white dress shirt, and certain shots of him wearing it reveal his collarbone in a manner more consistent with scenes of female models. Other styling choices in “Perfume” that seemingly oppose the “masculinity” portrayed in cologne commercials include the members’ use of necklaces and earrings; the usage of vivid colors, such as orange and purple; the multilayered, free-flowing outfit combinations; and the choice of certain fabrics, such as silk, mesh, and sequin. Aligning with the previously-mentioned “natural” aspirational image, which is associated with “femininity,” the video also heavily features floral imagery– the members carry flowers, dance in rooms surrounded by them, run while surrounded by falling petals, and even appear to be crafting perfumes derived from flowers, to name a few examples.
This portrayal, while differing from male-marketed fragrance marketing, aligns more with male performances of gender typically seen throughout the K-Pop industry. A common male image in the industry is the “Flower Boy,” a concept that “frames male idols with an effeminate appearance, typically adorned with makeup, jewelry, unisex clothing, and displays of vulnerability and liminal masculinity.” (qtd. in Bao and Wijitsopon, pg. 52) Even when men are featured at the center, in the K-Pop industry, the primary audience usually consists of women, making adapting influences from another industry to evolving female tastes a key component to success. This shift reflects how K-pop reshapes masculine imagery to appeal to a predominantly female audience, softening the dominance-driven masculinity typical of Western fragrance advertising.
While Western perfume commercials frequently make use of models, they also commonly feature celebrities, using the pre-existing associations surrounding that celebrity as a marketing tactic. In her paper “ Communicative Anosmia and Poetics of Storytelling in Perfume Commercials,” Halszka Leleń writes that perfume commercials “evidently capitalize on the multiple, polyphonic intertextual associations activated in addressee’s minds by seeing their favorite actresses or actors starring in a given film.” Within Perfume, these associations are capitalized on even further, as not only does each group member have his own distinct set of associated qualities, but strong, devoted parasocial relationships between fans and idols are the norm within the K-Pop industry. All three members are brand ambassadors for high-end fashion brands, with Jaehyun collaborating with Prada in 2022, Doyoung with Dolce & Gabbana in 2023, and Jungwoo with Tods’ in 2023 (Loh, et. al, 2026). These partnerships give the members an image of luxury, adding to the intended effect of the Perfume album’s promotional materials and music video. Parasocial relationships also play an important role in Perfume ’s creative and marketing strategies. Before re-debuting as a trio, the members of NCT DOJAEJUNG had spent several years as members of NCT 127, establishing on-screen dynamics as individuals, with each other, and with their fans. Fans already felt like they “knew” the members, giving the opportunity to create an even stronger response to seeing them “star” in film-like materials that portrayed them in romantic scenarios. With this method,, Perfume extends the logic of celebrity endorsement beyond recognition into sustained emotional attachment, a key distinction from traditional perfume advertising.



Beyond visual and celebrity-driven strategies, Perfume further deepens its engagement through multisensory and narrative techniques. Throughout the “Perfume” music video, a loose narrative centers around each of the members designing perfumes and collectively branding themselves as “Proust House,” twice delivering these perfumes to the doorstep of the female model briefly featured throughout the video. Aligning with this reference to French author Marcel Proust, whose novel In Search of Lost Time notably connected scent and taste to emotional memories, many of the lyrics throughout the Perfume album heavily draw upon this connection. Beginning with the first verse of “Perfume,” the titular track, which opens by immediately introducing fragrance-themed lines (“I’ll fill your day with heavenly scents / To remind you of my name at every step”), a significant amount of the song’s lyrics consist of metaphors linking together the qualities of perfume and emotional connections, particularly focusing on feelings of sensuality. The song’s chorus, in particular, (“Leaving ‘em on your wrist / On your collar / Gonna let ‘em hover / So you can’t forget me / Mixed in with the smallest sigh”) centers around making these connections. The lyrics first highlight the wrist and collar, two “pulse points” of the body where scent is marketed by many perfume companies to last the longest (“Pulse Points and Beyond”), in order to evoke the physical experience of applying perfume and tie in a layer of intimacy to it. Even the song’s dance choreography emphasizes this act, having the members deftly raise their wrists to their noses as they sing the associated lyrics. The preceding lyric then adds a layer of emotional significance to this physical experience by tying memories of the song’s speaker to the fragrance being applied, making sure that the applicant of the perfume “can’t forget” him. The final lyric further drives in this dual connection with a phrase open for interpretation, as a “smallest sigh” could emerge from a sense of satisfaction derived from a fragrance’s scent, or it could be a subtle expression of lovesickness.
This sensory strategy extends beyond scent alone. “Strawberry Sunday,” another song on Perfume, lyrically associates another sense– taste– to memories and feelings of intimacy. The song first recalls memories of a cherished night spent together, with the chorus going on to describe it as a “perfect Strawberry Sunday.” This association between a close memory and a sweet dessert (a strawberry sundae) brings together two seemingly unrelated sensory experiences, using the favorable feelings listeners likely have towards desserts to create and strengthen positive feelings with the singers. As the song progresses, the song clearly lays out this connection, with lyrics stating, “This emotion we feel is breakfast / A lovely kiss to open up both our days,” “It’s always new, at long last / Time for dessert,” “You taste so sweet,” “White clouds like ice cream,” and “Sunday, at the start and end of every week / I’m cravin’ you / Deeply melting honeydew.” Taste has become a growing aspect of perfume conceptualization, with a trend of “gourmand” (edible-scenting) fragrances carrying over into both Western and Korean perfume markets. “Strawberry Sunday” capitalizes on this trend, further expanding upon the album’s concept of sensory-based memory activation.
Another sensory-related aspect of the Perfume album was the creation of a perfume unique to each member. In a YouTube video titled “THE BLOTTER PAPER: Blending Process,” released on April 2, 2023, the group’s members describe the creative process behind the scent selection and naming of their fragrances. One member, Doyoung, describes his rationale, recounting his love for the “transition from winter to spring” and his desire to capture that phenomenon in a scent (NCT, 0:25-40). He also connects his scent choices to the role he plays in the group, stating, “I was thinking, ‘What would I look like as part of DOJAEJUNG?’…I want to be the most subtle part of this team.” (NCT, 1:31-2:06) These statements tie back to the practices of storytelling and memory-evocative scent within the Korean perfume industry, showcasing NCT DOJAEJUNG’s adept connection to this market. Among the multiple versions of the physical copy of the album, three of the versions shipped with a blotter paper containing the scents that the members described in this video (one version for each individual member’s fragrance). Fans now had the opportunity to experience each scent, creating new associations in between their perceptions of the members and these thoughtfully crafted scents– further heightening their parasocial connections.
These songs’ lyrics and the conception of the physical scent cards may be intentional attempts to evoke the “Proust Phenomenon,” which is an “ immediate and involuntary recall (‘cued recall’) through an odour-invoked memory.” (Groes and Mercer) Creating strong associations between scent (in the form of perfume) and emotional memories is not only the goal of this album, but the goal of the perfume industry as a whole, putting NCT DOJAEJUNG’s Perfume in alignment with the industry whose aesthetics and marketing tactics it set out to emulate and adapt to the K-pop market.



Going back to Lee Soo-man’s proposed concept of “cultural technology,” the intersection in Perfume of two highly aesthetically-based mediums that carry across cultural implications (perfume commercials and K-Pop albums) adds up more, both creatively and financially. Adapting Western influences with a Korean lens aids in spreading Korean culture across the globe while also appealing towards regional preferences, a key goal of the “cultural technology” system. As K-Pop continues to expand globally, more potential opportunities lie in crafting creative concepts fusing together global and domestic industries. Several other K-Pop concepts, even within NCT, have taken direct influences from outside industries– the designer car aesthetic used throughout 2 Baddies by NCT 127, the hockey player concept in the music video for “90s Love” by NCT U, and the martial arts movie aesthetic in the “Kick It” music video by NCT 127, to name a few examples. However, fewer examples exist that fully commit to adapting an external inspiration in the thorough way that Perfume does, which will make any future attempts interesting to observe.



Bao, Jiayin, and Raksangob, Wijitsopon. “‘‘Fierce Girls, Flower Guys’: The Discursive Representation of Gender Identities through English in K-pop Lyrics.” LEARN Journal: Language Education and Acquisition Research Network, vol. 18, no. 2, 31 July 2025, pp. 47–77, https://doi.org/10.70730/fnhu2224.
This academic article analyzes how gender identities are constructed within K-pop lyrics, particularly through the use of English and recurring archetypes such as the “flower boy.” The authors argue that K-pop frequently presents softer, more fluid forms of masculinity that diverge from traditional Western norms. As a peer-reviewed source, it provides a credible theoretical framework for understanding gender representation within the industry. I used this source to support my analysis of how Perfume subverts masculine conventions commonly seen in Western fragrance commercials, basing my observations in established academic research on K-pop gender performance.
Benjamin, Jeff. “NCT’s Doyoung, Jaehyun & Jungwoo Team up for New Dojaejung Trio.” Billboard, Billboard, 28 Mar. 2023, www.billboard.com/music/pop/nct-doyoung-jaehyun-jungwoo-team-up-dojaejung-trio-12 35293601/.
This article reports on the formation of NCT DOJAEJUNG and provides background on the group’s debut and promotional rollout. As a piece from Billboard, it is a reliable industry source that offers factual information about release timelines and group structure. I used this source primarily to establish context for the group’s debut and to support my discussion of teaser releases and promotional strategies within the K-pop industry.
Choi, Jeong-Yoon. “Scents and Sensibility: How Perfumes Are Redefining K-Beauty.” The Korea Herald, The Korea Herald, 29 Mar. 2025, www.koreaherald.com/article/10451693
This article explores how contemporary Korean perfume brands emphasize emotional connection and memory in their marketing strategies. It includes industry insights and direct quotes, such as Vicky Jung’s description of fragrance as a medium for preserving memories. As a journalistic source focused on cultural trends, it is useful for understanding current shifts in the Korean fragrance market. I used this source to support my claim that Korean perfume marketing prioritizes storytelling and emotional resonance, which directly connects to Perfume ’s thematic focus.
Groes, Sebastian, and R.M. Francis. Smell, Memory, and Literature in the Black Country. Palgrave Macmillan, 2021.
This scholarly book examines the relationship between scent, memory, and literature, including the concept of involuntary memory recall triggered by smell. It provides a theoretical basis for understanding the “Proust Phenomenon.” As an academic source, it is highly credible and grounded in literary and sensory studies. I used this source to define and contextualize the idea of scent-triggered memory, helping frame my analysis of how Perfume attempts to evoke similar responses through its lyrics and concept.
Jackson, Julie. “SM Founder Talks New Boy Band, Future Localization Strategy.” The Korea Herald, The Korea Herald, 28 Jan. 2016, www.koreaherald.com/article/835521.
This article discusses SM Entertainment’s global expansion strategy and introduces the concept behind NCT’s initial “limitless” structure. Published in The Korea Herald, it provides credible insight into the company’s intentions and industry positioning. I used this source to explain the conceptual foundation of NCT and to support my discussion of “cultural technology” and globalization within K-pop.
Kim, S. “NCT Dojaejung’s Debut Album ‘Perfume’ Breaks Unit Group Record for 1st-Week Sales.” Soompi, 24 Apr. 2023, www.soompi.com/article/1582246wpp/nct-dojaejungs-debut-album-perfume-breaks-unit- group-record-for-1st-week-sales.
This article reports on the commercial success of NCT DOJAEJUNG’s Perfume album, including its record-breaking first-week sales. As a K-pop news source, it is useful for providing up-to-date industry data. It also cites Naver, a reliable Korean news agency, as a source, providing further credibility. I used this source to demonstrate the group’s commercial impact and to reinforce the effectiveness of their promotional and conceptual strategies.
Kim, Yoon-ji. “How Lee Soo-Man’s Idol System at SM Paved the Way for K-Pop as We Know It.” Hankyoreh, 23 Mar. 2023, english.hani.co.kr/arti/english_edition/e_entertainment/1084898.html.
This article examines Lee Soo-man’s influence on the K-pop industry and the development of SM Entertainment’s trainee system. It provides historical and analytical context for how the industry operates. As a journalistic source with a focus on industry history, it is reliable for background information. I used this source to explain the structural foundations of K-pop and to support my discussion of “cultural technology.”
Klove, Laurence Arrigo. “The Stellar Rise of Korean Fragrances Has Rich Cultural References.” Essencional, 25 June 2025, www.essencional.com/en/posts/the-stellar-rise-of-korean-fragrances-has-rich-cultural-ref erences/
This article outlines the historical use of fragrance in Korea, including its origins in ritual practices. It connects traditional uses of scent to modern developments in the Korean fragrance industry. As a cultural analysis piece, it offers useful historical context, although it is less formal than academic or journalistic sources. I used this source briefly to establish the long-standing cultural significance of fragrance in Korea.
“K Perfume from Niche to Mainstream: The Rise of Korean Perfume.” Carvansons, 22 Jan. 2026, www.carvansons.co.uk/k-perfume-from-niche-to-mainstream-the-rise-of-korean-perfume /.
This article discusses the rapid growth of the Korean perfume industry and highlights emerging brands and trends. It emphasizes the shift toward unique scent profiles and storytelling-driven marketing. While it is a general industry article rather than a scholarly source, it provides relevant and current examples. I used this source to support my discussion of newer Korean perfume brands and their distinctive approaches.
Leleń, Halszka. “Communicative Anosmia and Poetics of Storytelling in Perfume Commercials.” Media, Biznes, Kultura, vol. 2, no. 13, 2022, pp. 27-41. ProQuest, https://proxy.emerson.edu/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/scholarly-journals/comm unicative-anosmia-poetics-storytelling/docview/2934033194/se-2, doi:https://doi.org/10.4467/25442554.MBK.22.013.17099.
This academic article analyzes how perfume commercials communicate meaning despite the absence of scent, focusing on storytelling and celebrity associations. It introduces the concept of “communicative anosmia” and highlights the role of pre-existing viewer connections with celebrity appearances. As a peer-reviewed source, it provides a strong theoretical basis. I used this source to support my discussion of celebrity usage in perfume advertising and to compare it with parasocial dynamics in K-pop.
Lin, Deng, and Shi Huina. “The False Obvious: Gender Myth in Perfume Commercials.” Cultural and Religious Studies, vol. 12, no. 1, Jan. 2024, pp. 8–14, https://doi.org/ 10.17265/2328-2177/2024.01.002.
This scholarly article examines gender representation and visual conventions in perfume commercials, including themes of luxury, race, and masculinity. It provides both qualitative analysis and statistical observations. As an academic source, it is credible and directly relevant to my topic. I used this source in my analysis of Western perfume advertising, specifically in discussions of aspirational imagery and gender norms.
Loh, Ynez, et al. “The A-Z of Korean Brand Ambassadors.” Harper’s Bazaar Singapore, 9 Apr. 2026, www.harpersbazaar.com.sg/celebrity/from-jimin-to-ji-chang-wook-see-which-korean-star s-are-designer-brand-ambassadors.
This article compiles Korean celebrities who serve as brand ambassadors for luxury fashion houses. It provides examples of specific partnerships between idols and global brands. As a fashion media source, it is useful for identifying industry trends I used this source to support my point about NCT DOJAEJUNG members’ luxury brand affiliations and how these associations contribute to their image.
“NCT DOJAEJUNG 엔시티 도재정 ‘Perfume’ MV” Youtube, Uploaded by SMTOWN, 17 Apr. 2023, https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7nRiE4sLLxE
This music video, featuring the song “Perfume” by NCT DOJAEJUNG (a sub-unit of NCT), was released under SM Entertainment and directed by 2ee.hyein. Filmed in Thailand, it combines dance performance with stylized “lifestyle” scenes and narrative elements. As the primary text of my analysis, it serves as the central object through which I examine visual, thematic, and marketing techniques.
“Perfume Market Size, Share & Industry Analysis, By Type (Perfume, Eau de Perfume, Eau de Toilette, Eau de Cologne, and Eau Fraiche), By Product (Mass and Premium), By End-user (Men and Women), By Distribution Channel (Online and Offline), and Regional Forecast, 2026-2034.” Fortune Business Insights. 13 April 2026. https://www.fortunebusinessinsights.com/perfume-market-102273
This industry report provides data on the global perfume market, including market size and growth projections. As a market research source, it is useful for establishing the economic scale of the perfume industry. I used this source in my introduction to contextualize the commercial significance of perfume and justify its relevance as a subject of analysis.
“Pulse Points and beyond: The Guide on Where to Spray Perfume.” Eight & Bob, 2 Sept. 2025, eightandbob.com/blogs/eight-bob-journal/pulse-points-and-beyond-the-definitive-guide-o n-where-to-spray-perfume?srsltid=AfmBOoqknepVZnpQ3SdcvNVK3aovs0gk3rbOmW QuMwZxBv0XOY6udJ8G
This article, published by a perfume company, explains where perfume is typically applied on the body and why certain areas enhance scent longevity. While it is a practical guide rather than an academic source, it offers useful insight into how perfume companies market perfume usage practices. I used this source briefly to support my analysis of lyrical references to “pulse points” in “Perfume.”
Oak, Jessica. “The Root of K-Pop: The Influences of Today’s Biggest Acts.” Billboard, Billboard, 12 Nov. 2013, www.billboard.com/music/music-news/the-root-of-k-pop-the-influences-of-todays-bigges t-acts-5638224/
This article explores the origins and influences of K-pop, identifying key moments such as the debut of Seo Taiji and Boys. As a Billboard article, it provides accessible yet credible historical context. I used this source to establish the early foundations of the K-pop industry.
Santos, Ally. “The Rise of Scent Culture in South Korea.” PLEZURO, 25 May 2025, plezuromag.com/news/the-rise-of-scent-culture-in-south-korea.
This article discusses the rise of scent culture in South Korea and highlights changing consumer preferences. It focuses on the increasing importance of emotional and cultural connections in fragrance marketing. As a trend-focused piece, it is useful for understanding contemporary developments. I used this source to support my discussion of shifts in Korean perfume market preferences.
Schwarcz, Joe. “The Story of Perfume.” McGill Office for Science and Society, McGill University, 10 July 2017, www.mcgill.ca/oss/article/history/story-perfume.
This article provides a historical overview of perfume, tracing its roots back to ancient civilizations. As a science communication piece from McGill University, it is reliable for general background information. I used this source in my introduction to establish the long history of perfume.

title: Natalie Tsutsumi Final Paper
text: With a rich history tracing back to ancient Mesopotamia (Scharcz), the creation of perfume has evolved over thousands of years and expanded rapidly within the past two centuries. As the potential for profitability within this market increased, alongside with the overall technological advancements in film technology, the marketing campaigns of perfumes shifted towards commercial films, which grew to have a distinct set of conventions. Even with the absence of smell in any of these commercials– the main selling point of a perfume– they have managed to effectively entice viewers to buy their products, with a global market size of 53.04 billion USD in 2025 (Fortune Business Insights). American and European companies dominate among the overall top perfume brands (Fortune Business Insights), with established luxury brands crafting campaigns appealing to multisensory experiences and aspirational lifestyles. Within South Korea, a rising market for the perfume industry, Korean fragrance brands have emerged with original appeals to the “story behind the scent” (Santos) and an “emotional connection”, emphasizing fragrance’s ties to personal memories (Choi). Perfume, an album released by K-pop group NCT DOJAEJUNG in April 2023, explored these ties through its fragrance-centered creative concept, taking influences from both domestic and global perfume industries. This paper will analyze how Perfume, specifically through the music video of its titular single “Perfume”, its album video teasers, and its physical album packaging, brought together many of the most compelling marketing and stylistic techniques of the perfume and K-Pop industries. Fusions of commercial and artistic values take place all throughout the music industry, yet Perfume serves as a uniquely concentrated example of this delicate balance, effectively adapting the personal connection aspects of the perfume industry to the highly fan connection-reliant K-Pop medium. This hybridization further blurs the already unclear boundary between the two values, filtering the aesthetic of perfume consumption into an emotional experience.

title: Natalie Tsutsumi Final Paper
text:
The origins of the K-Pop industry can be traced back to the 1990s, with the 1992 debut of hip-hop trio Seo Taji and Boys commonly acknowledged as the “definitive genesis of K-Pop” (Oak). SM Entertainment, one of the largest and most influential K-Pop agencies, is credited for introducing the “trainee system” (in which potential idols train for years before being selected to debut in a group) to the K-Pop industry after its founding in 1989 (Kim). Soo-man Lee, the founder and former executive producer of SM Entertainment, described his system for artistic development and global expansion as “cultural technology.” In a 2011 address at Stanford Business School, he explained that he saw culture as a “type of technology” that is “much more exquisite and complex” than the “information technology” of the nineties. The guidelines created at SM Entertainment for adapting “cultural technology” to targeted countries were precisely narrowed down to specifics, including optimal chord progressions, eyeshadow shades, camera angles in music videos, and hand gestures (Seabrook).
Lee continued to set out to expand upon his vision of “cultural technology.” In January 2016, he held a press conference where he announced the debut of SM Entertainment’s latest idol group. NCT, which stands for Neo Culture Technology, would have an “limitless” number of members, as it would be a group consisting of multiple subunits based in cities all around the world. (Jackson) These aspirations mostly failed to materialize, as all of the debuted subunits (NCT U, NCT 127, NCT Dream, NCT DOJAEJUNG, NCT JNJM, NCT Wish) apart from one (WayV) are based in South Korea; additionally, the end of the “limitless” member concept was marked with the 2023 debut of NCT Wish, the planned “final” unit of NCT (Benjamin). Nevertheless, throughout the past decade, each unit of NCT has merged a unique blend of global influences with Korean culture, creating a highly successful brand that has pushed its “neo” creative concepts forward.
NCT DOJAEJUNG, a subunit of NCT, debuted in April 2023 with the mini-album Perfume. The three members (Doyoung, Jaehyun, and Jungwoo) had already debuted within NCT, all being members of the NCT 127 unit. Following the official announcement of the group’s debut on March 22, 2023 (Benjamin), up until the album’s release, the group unveiled a set of photo and video teasers, a standard practice within the K-Pop industry. On April 17, 2023, the group released the music video for the titular single “Perfume,” along with the rest of the album tracks. The album sold over 672,300 copies in its first week, “setting a new record [in South Korea] for the most number of albums sold in the first week for a unit after the album’s release.” (Kim)
Within South Korea, fragrance has been utilized for thousands of years, with their use in folk rituals for spiritual purification dating back to 500 B.C. (Klove) Moving towards the present, Korean perfume brands have been rising amongst the larger Korean beauty industry, finding their place through unique scents and intentional connections between scent and memories. While previous generations preferred more “clean” and “fresh” scents, such as florals and citruses, (Santos) new brands are emerging, appealing to younger generations with bold scents tied to Korean culture. One example is BORNTOSTANDOUT, a brand founded in 2022 that offers unconventional scent notes in its unisex fragrances, like steamed rice and overripe banana. (“K-Perfume”). Another popular Korean brand, Tamburins, (endorsed by K-Pop idols Jennie from BLACKPINK and Felix from Stray Kids) combines striking packaging and elaborate architectural installations with standout scents. One of its popular fragrances, “Pumkini,” combines the scents of white pumpkin, perilla leaves, and blood oranges (Scipioni). Other brands lean more towards storytelling aspects, deliberately crafting scents that evoke specific memories. Nonfiction, a brand launched in 2019, designs scents around feelings or memories, aiming to “capture mood and atmosphere rather than [follow] conventional Western fragrance structures.” (“K-Perfume”) Vicky Jung, founder of the Seoul-based perfume collection Deacoutre, perfectly encapsulates this approach towards perfume in a written interview with The Korea Herald, stating, “Fragrance is a special language of preserving memories, of anchoring emotions in time…We remember scents more deeply than we remember faces or words. That’s why perfume matters — it lingers, even when everything else fades.” (Choi) With an estimated 2025 market size of $754.71 million (“South Korea Perfume”), the Korean perfume industry is only expected to grow, with these innovative and thoughtful approaches pushing it forward domestically and internationally and influencing the creation of NCT DOJAEJUNG’s Perfume.

title: Natalie Tsutsumi Final Paper
text:
While the music video for “Perfume” takes inspiration from a wide range of aspects of perfume commercials, two of the clearest areas of influence can be seen in its use of aspirational imagery and its reworking of gender conventions. Displays of aspirational lifestyles are a key aspect of Western perfume commercials. These displays frequently fall into two main categories: fantasies of luxurious lifestyles, and fantasies of natural lifestyles. The “luxury” themed commercials often feature extravagant imagery and settings, including castles with chandeliers, opulent parties, swimming pools, car drives, and sprawling cityscapes. In “The False Obvious: Gender Myth in Perfume Commercials,” a 2024 academic article written by Den Lin and Shi Huina, they note that these visuals serve to “demonstrate the dignity and superiority of the sophisticated and dreamy Western middle class.” Images of “luxury” also typically contain Western ideals of racial ideals. Lin and Huina also note that within the 45 randomly selected perfume commercials they studied, 95% contained white actors, a key aspect of “producing the ‘conservative Englishness look.’” (qtd. by Lin and Huina) This aligns with their observed tendency for Western perfume commercials to be “less localized” than other categories of commercials. (Lin and Huina). While still conforming to these ideals, “natural” themed perfume commercials appeal to consumers through aspirations of reconnecting with “desirable” aspects of nature through their fragrance. These commercials frequently use floral imagery, a key aspect of this approach. Notable examples include commercials for Gucci’s “Bloom” and “Flora” fragrances, which all contain floral imagery conveying associations with freshness and simplicity– but with an elevated twist. Most “natural” themed perfume commercials contain elements of aspirational luxury to some degree, and many combine them with more explicitly luxurious elements, such as yachts, to elements of nature, like ocean destinations.
The “Perfume” music video by NCT DOJAEJUNG contains elements of both of these categories, with an approach leaning more towards the “nature” themed aspirational displays. Many similar shots to nature-centric perfume commercials can be seen in “Perfume,” several of which are displayed below:



Figure — collage from the “Perfume” music video


These familiar conventions provided “Perfume” with an established framework to adapt, allowing it to convey a “refreshing” image– a valued trait within the K-Pop industry. Themes of aspirational luxury also play a role in “Perfume,” yet with a slightly different approach from Western perfume commercials. The music video was entirely recorded in Thailand, a choice in setting departing from the typical European destinations. Many of the scenes take place inside a rustic-style indoor dome, which aligns more with a “nature” centric image; however, other scenes take place outside a luxurious, European-style mansion, which comes complete with a swimming pool that the members dive into– showcasing themes of released inhibitions, freedom, and youth. Furthermore, NCT DOJAEJUNG, the protagonists of the “Perfume” music video, are all Asian, breaking in tradition with the typically white actor seen in perfume commercials. However, the music video also (briefly) features a female model, who is the receiver of the perfumes designed by the members. This model, shown in the collage above, is white, visually matching the common protagonist of a Western perfume commercial. The combination of these choices help distinguish “Perfume” as a product within an Asian industry, while also keeping it in alignment with a Western industry it sets out to emulate.
Western fragrance commercials frequently exhibit a gendered set of conventions, both towards men and women. Within male-marketed commercials, an attractive male model or celebrity tends to be featured, who is often shirtless or in minimal clothing that reveals a chiseled, muscular form. Other commercials showcase men wearing outfits commonly deemed as “masculine,” such as black leather jackets, white dress shirts, and navy suits. The contents of many of these commercials can be categorized into two categories: those showing the men participating in strenuous physical activities, giving the cologne an association with masculine power and strength; and those showing the men being desired by women and engaging in sexuality with them, giving the cologne an association with desirability and sexual dominance. Male-marketed fragrance commercials also typically refrain from floral imagery and the use of bright colors, especially shades of purple and pink.
“Perfume”, however, subverts many of these conventions, framing its male subjects in a “softer” manner more typical of a female-marketed perfume commercial. None of the members display muscular forms or wear any of the aforementioned typically “masculine” outfits; only member Doyoung dons a cotton white dress shirt, and certain shots of him wearing it reveal his collarbone in a manner more consistent with scenes of female models. Other styling choices in “Perfume” that seemingly oppose the “masculinity” portrayed in cologne commercials include the members’ use of necklaces and earrings; the usage of vivid colors, such as orange and purple; the multilayered, free-flowing outfit combinations; and the choice of certain fabrics, such as silk, mesh, and sequin. Aligning with the previously-mentioned “natural” aspirational image, which is associated with “femininity,” the video also heavily features floral imagery– the members carry flowers, dance in rooms surrounded by them, run while surrounded by falling petals, and even appear to be crafting perfumes derived from flowers, to name a few examples.
This portrayal, while differing from male-marketed fragrance marketing, aligns more with male performances of gender typically seen throughout the K-Pop industry. A common male image in the industry is the “Flower Boy,” a concept that “frames male idols with an effeminate appearance, typically adorned with makeup, jewelry, unisex clothing, and displays of vulnerability and liminal masculinity.” (qtd. in Bao and Wijitsopon, pg. 52) Even when men are featured at the center, in the K-Pop industry, the primary audience usually consists of women, making adapting influences from another industry to evolving female tastes a key component to success. This shift reflects how K-pop reshapes masculine imagery to appeal to a predominantly female audience, softening the dominance-driven masculinity typical of Western fragrance advertising.
While Western perfume commercials frequently make use of models, they also commonly feature celebrities, using the pre-existing associations surrounding that celebrity as a marketing tactic. In her paper “ Communicative Anosmia and Poetics of Storytelling in Perfume Commercials,” Halszka Leleń writes that perfume commercials “evidently capitalize on the multiple, polyphonic intertextual associations activated in addressee’s minds by seeing their favorite actresses or actors starring in a given film.” Within Perfume, these associations are capitalized on even further, as not only does each group member have his own distinct set of associated qualities, but strong, devoted parasocial relationships between fans and idols are the norm within the K-Pop industry. All three members are brand ambassadors for high-end fashion brands, with Jaehyun collaborating with Prada in 2022, Doyoung with Dolce & Gabbana in 2023, and Jungwoo with Tods’ in 2023 (Loh, et. al, 2026). These partnerships give the members an image of luxury, adding to the intended effect of the Perfume album’s promotional materials and music video. Parasocial relationships also play an important role in Perfume ’s creative and marketing strategies. Before re-debuting as a trio, the members of NCT DOJAEJUNG had spent several years as members of NCT 127, establishing on-screen dynamics as individuals, with each other, and with their fans. Fans already felt like they “knew” the members, giving the opportunity to create an even stronger response to seeing them “star” in film-like materials that portrayed them in romantic scenarios. With this method,, Perfume extends the logic of celebrity endorsement beyond recognition into sustained emotional attachment, a key distinction from traditional perfume advertising.

title: Natalie Tsutsumi Final Paper
text:
Beyond visual and celebrity-driven strategies, Perfume further deepens its engagement through multisensory and narrative techniques. Throughout the “Perfume” music video, a loose narrative centers around each of the members designing perfumes and collectively branding themselves as “Proust House,” twice delivering these perfumes to the doorstep of the female model briefly featured throughout the video. Aligning with this reference to French author Marcel Proust, whose novel In Search of Lost Time notably connected scent and taste to emotional memories, many of the lyrics throughout the Perfume album heavily draw upon this connection. Beginning with the first verse of “Perfume,” the titular track, which opens by immediately introducing fragrance-themed lines (“I’ll fill your day with heavenly scents / To remind you of my name at every step”), a significant amount of the song’s lyrics consist of metaphors linking together the qualities of perfume and emotional connections, particularly focusing on feelings of sensuality. The song’s chorus, in particular, (“Leaving ‘em on your wrist / On your collar / Gonna let ‘em hover / So you can’t forget me / Mixed in with the smallest sigh”) centers around making these connections. The lyrics first highlight the wrist and collar, two “pulse points” of the body where scent is marketed by many perfume companies to last the longest (“Pulse Points and Beyond”), in order to evoke the physical experience of applying perfume and tie in a layer of intimacy to it. Even the song’s dance choreography emphasizes this act, having the members deftly raise their wrists to their noses as they sing the associated lyrics. The preceding lyric then adds a layer of emotional significance to this physical experience by tying memories of the song’s speaker to the fragrance being applied, making sure that the applicant of the perfume “can’t forget” him. The final lyric further drives in this dual connection with a phrase open for interpretation, as a “smallest sigh” could emerge from a sense of satisfaction derived from a fragrance’s scent, or it could be a subtle expression of lovesickness.
This sensory strategy extends beyond scent alone. “Strawberry Sunday,” another song on Perfume, lyrically associates another sense– taste– to memories and feelings of intimacy. The song first recalls memories of a cherished night spent together, with the chorus going on to describe it as a “perfect Strawberry Sunday.” This association between a close memory and a sweet dessert (a strawberry sundae) brings together two seemingly unrelated sensory experiences, using the favorable feelings listeners likely have towards desserts to create and strengthen positive feelings with the singers. As the song progresses, the song clearly lays out this connection, with lyrics stating, “This emotion we feel is breakfast / A lovely kiss to open up both our days,” “It’s always new, at long last / Time for dessert,” “You taste so sweet,” “White clouds like ice cream,” and “Sunday, at the start and end of every week / I’m cravin’ you / Deeply melting honeydew.” Taste has become a growing aspect of perfume conceptualization, with a trend of “gourmand” (edible-scenting) fragrances carrying over into both Western and Korean perfume markets. “Strawberry Sunday” capitalizes on this trend, further expanding upon the album’s concept of sensory-based memory activation.
Another sensory-related aspect of the Perfume album was the creation of a perfume unique to each member. In a YouTube video titled “THE BLOTTER PAPER: Blending Process,” released on April 2, 2023, the group’s members describe the creative process behind the scent selection and naming of their fragrances. One member, Doyoung, describes his rationale, recounting his love for the “transition from winter to spring” and his desire to capture that phenomenon in a scent (NCT, 0:25-40). He also connects his scent choices to the role he plays in the group, stating, “I was thinking, ‘What would I look like as part of DOJAEJUNG?’…I want to be the most subtle part of this team.” (NCT, 1:31-2:06) These statements tie back to the practices of storytelling and memory-evocative scent within the Korean perfume industry, showcasing NCT DOJAEJUNG’s adept connection to this market. Among the multiple versions of the physical copy of the album, three of the versions shipped with a blotter paper containing the scents that the members described in this video (one version for each individual member’s fragrance). Fans now had the opportunity to experience each scent, creating new associations in between their perceptions of the members and these thoughtfully crafted scents– further heightening their parasocial connections.
These songs’ lyrics and the conception of the physical scent cards may be intentional attempts to evoke the “Proust Phenomenon,” which is an “ immediate and involuntary recall (‘cued recall’) through an odour-invoked memory.” (Groes and Mercer) Creating strong associations between scent (in the form of perfume) and emotional memories is not only the goal of this album, but the goal of the perfume industry as a whole, putting NCT DOJAEJUNG’s Perfume in alignment with the industry whose aesthetics and marketing tactics it set out to emulate and adapt to the K-pop market.

title: Natalie Tsutsumi Final Paper
text: Going back to Lee Soo-man’s proposed concept of “cultural technology,” the intersection in Perfume of two highly aesthetically-based mediums that carry across cultural implications (perfume commercials and K-Pop albums) adds up more, both creatively and financially. Adapting Western influences with a Korean lens aids in spreading Korean culture across the globe while also appealing towards regional preferences, a key goal of the “cultural technology” system. As K-Pop continues to expand globally, more potential opportunities lie in crafting creative concepts fusing together global and domestic industries. Several other K-Pop concepts, even within NCT, have taken direct influences from outside industries– the designer car aesthetic used throughout 2 Baddies by NCT 127, the hockey player concept in the music video for “90s Love” by NCT U, and the martial arts movie aesthetic in the “Kick It” music video by NCT 127, to name a few examples. However, fewer examples exist that fully commit to adapting an external inspiration in the thorough way that Perfume does, which will make any future attempts interesting to observe.

This article explores the origins and influences of K-pop, identifying key moments such as the debut of Seo Taiji and Boys. As a Billboard article, it provides accessible yet credible historical context. I used this source to establish the early foundations of the K-pop industry.

This article discusses the rise of scent culture in South Korea and highlights changing consumer preferences. It focuses on the increasing importance of emotional and cultural connections in fragrance marketing. As a trend-focused piece, it is useful for understanding contemporary developments. I used this source to support my discussion of shifts in Korean perfume market preferences.

This article provides a historical overview of perfume, tracing its roots back to ancient civilizations. As a science communication piece from McGill University, it is reliable for general background information. I used this source in my introduction to establish the long history of perfume.

This article examines how Korean fragrance brands, particularly Tamburins, are influencing the global perfume industry through design and innovation. It highlights the importance of aesthetics and branding. As a fashion and design-focused source, it provides relevant examples. It focuses particularly on SM Entertainment and its role in shaping the industry’s global expansion. As a detailed and well-researched journalistic source, it provides valuable insight into “cultural technology.” I used this source to support my discussion of SM Entertainment’s influence and the strategic construction of K-pop groups.

As a primary source from the group’s label, it offers useful insight into the album’s concept, though it presents it in a promotional context. title: Perfume
text:
Introduction
With a rich history tracing back to ancient Mesopotamia (Schwarcz 2017), the creation of perfume has evolved over thousands of years and expanded rapidly within the past two centuries. As the potential for profitability within this market increased, alongside with the overall technological advancements in film technology, the marketing campaigns of perfumes shifted towards commercial films, which grew to have a distinct set of conventions. Even with the absence of smell in any of these commercials– the main selling point of a perfume– they have managed to effectively entice viewers to buy their products, with a global market size of 53.04 billion USD in 2025 (Fortune Business Insights 2026). American and European companies dominate among the overall top perfume brands (Fortune Business Insights 2026), with established luxury brands crafting campaigns appealing to multisensory experiences and aspirational lifestyles. Within South Korea, a rising market for the perfume industry, Korean fragrance brands have emerged with original appeals to the “story behind the scent” (Santos 2025) and an “emotional connection”, emphasizing fragrance’s ties to personal memories (Choi 2025). Perfume, an album released by K-pop group NCT DOJAEJUNG in April 2023, explored these ties through its fragrance-centered creative concept, taking influences from both domestic and global perfume industries. This paper will analyze how Perfume, specifically through the music video of its titular single “Perfume”, its album video teasers, and its physical album packaging, brought together many of the most compelling marketing and stylistic techniques of the perfume and K-Pop industries. Fusions of commercial and artistic values take place all throughout the music industry, yet Perfume serves as a uniquely concentrated example of this delicate balance, effectively adapting the personal connection aspects of the perfume industry to the highly fan connection-reliant K-Pop medium. This hybridization further blurs the already unclear boundary between the two values, filtering the aesthetic of perfume consumption into an emotional experience.

title: Perfume
text:
Industry Contexts
The origins of the K-Pop industry can be traced back to the 1990s, with the 1992 debut of hip-hop trio Seo Taji and Boys commonly acknowledged as the “definitive genesis of K-Pop” (Oak 2013). SM Entertainment, one of the largest and most influential K-Pop agencies, is credited for introducing the “trainee system” (in which potential idols train for years before being selected to debut in a group) to the K-Pop industry after its founding in 1989 (Kim 2023). Soo-man Lee, the founder and former executive producer of SM Entertainment, described his system for artistic development and global expansion as “cultural technology.” In a 2011 address at Stanford Business School, he explained that he saw culture as a “type of technology” that is “much more exquisite and complex” than the “information technology” of the nineties. The guidelines created at SM Entertainment for adapting “cultural technology” to targeted countries were precisely narrowed down to specifics, including optimal chord progressions, eyeshadow shades, camera angles in music videos, and hand gestures (Seabrook 2012).
Lee continued to set out to expand upon his vision of “cultural technology.” In January 2016, he held a press conference where he announced the debut of SM Entertainment’s latest idol group. NCT, which stands for Neo Culture Technology, would have an “limitless” number of members, as it would be a group consisting of multiple subunits based in cities all around the world. (Jackson 2016) These aspirations mostly failed to materialize, as all of the debuted subunits (NCT U, NCT 127, NCT Dream, NCT DOJAEJUNG, NCT JNJM, NCT Wish) apart from one (WayV) are based in South Korea; additionally, the end of the “limitless” member concept was marked with the 2023 debut of NCT Wish, the planned “final” unit of NCT (Benjamin 2023). Nevertheless, throughout the past decade, each unit of NCT has merged a unique blend of global influences with Korean culture, creating a highly successful brand that has pushed its “neo” creative concepts forward.
NCT DOJAEJUNG, a subunit of NCT, debuted in April 2023 with the mini-album Perfume. The three members (Doyoung, Jaehyun, and Jungwoo) had already debuted within NCT, all being members of the NCT 127 unit. Following the official announcement of the group’s debut on March 22, 2023 (Benjamin), up until the album’s release, the group unveiled a set of photo and video teasers, a standard practice within the K-Pop industry. On April 17, 2023, the group released the music video for the titular single “Perfume,” along with the rest of the album tracks. The album sold over 672,300 copies in its first week, “setting a new record [in South Korea] for the most number of albums sold in the first week for a unit after the album’s release.” (Kim 2023)
Within South Korea, fragrance has been utilized for thousands of years, with their use in folk rituals for spiritual purification dating back to 500 B.C. (Klove 2025) Moving towards the present, Korean perfume brands have been rising amongst the larger Korean beauty industry, finding their place through unique scents and intentional connections between scent and memories. While previous generations preferred more “clean” and “fresh” scents, such as florals and citruses, (Santos 2025) new brands are emerging, appealing to younger generations with bold scents tied to Korean culture. One example is BORNTOSTANDOUT, a brand founded in 2022 that offers unconventional scent notes in its unisex fragrances, like steamed rice and overripe banana. (“K-Perfume”). Another popular Korean brand, Tamburins, (endorsed by K-Pop idols Jennie from BLACKPINK and Felix from Stray Kids) combines striking packaging and elaborate architectural installations with standout scents. One of its popular fragrances, “Pumkini,” combines the scents of white pumpkin, perilla leaves, and blood oranges (Scipioni 2024). Other brands lean more towards storytelling aspects, deliberately crafting scents that evoke specific memories. Nonfiction, a brand launched in 2019, designs scents around feelings or memories, aiming to “capture mood and atmosphere rather than [follow] conventional Western fragrance structures.” (“K-Perfume”) Vicky Jung, founder of the Seoul-based perfume collection Deacoutre, perfectly encapsulates this approach towards perfume in a written interview with The Korea Herald, stating, “Fragrance is a special language of preserving memories, of anchoring emotions in time…We remember scents more deeply than we remember faces or words. That’s why perfume matters — it lingers, even when everything else fades.” (Choi 2025) With an estimated 2025 market size of $754.71 million (“South Korea Perfume” 2025), the Korean perfume industry is only expected to grow, with these innovative and thoughtful approaches pushing it forward domestically and internationally and influencing the creation of NCT DOJAEJUNG’s Perfume.

title: Perfume
section: Influences & Variations From Western Perfume Commercials Within “Perfume”
text:
Influences & Variations From Western Perfume Commercials Within “Perfume”
While the music video for “Perfume” takes inspiration from a wide range of aspects of perfume commercials, two of the clearest areas of influence can be seen in its use of aspirational imagery and its reworking of gender conventions. Displays of aspirational lifestyles are a key aspect of Western perfume commercials. These displays frequently fall into two main categories: fantasies of luxurious lifestyles, and fantasies of natural lifestyles. The “luxury” themed commercials often feature extravagant imagery and settings, including castles with chandeliers, opulent parties, swimming pools, car drives, and sprawling cityscapes. In “The False Obvious: Gender Myth in Perfume Commercials,” a 2024 academic article written by Den Lin and Shi Huina, they note that these visuals serve to “demonstrate the dignity and superiority of the sophisticated and dreamy Western middle class.” Images of “luxury” also typically contain Western ideals of racial ideals. Lin and Huina also note that within the 45 randomly selected perfume commercials they studied, 95% contained white actors, a key aspect of “producing the ‘conservative Englishness look’” (qtd. in Lin and Huina 2024). This aligns with their observed tendency for Western perfume commercials to be “less localized” than other categories of commercials. (Lin and Huina 2024). While still conforming to these ideals, “natural” themed perfume commercials appeal to consumers through aspirations of reconnecting with “desirable” aspects of nature through their fragrance. These commercials frequently use floral imagery, a key aspect of this approach. Notable examples include commercials for Gucci’s “Bloom” and “Flora” fragrances, which all contain floral imagery conveying associations with freshness and simplicity– but with an elevated twist. Most “natural” themed perfume commercials contain elements of aspirational luxury to some degree, and many combine them with more explicitly luxurious elements, such as yachts, to elements of nature, like ocean destinations.
The “Perfume” music video by NCT DOJAEJUNG contains elements of both of these categories, with an approach leaning more towards the “nature” themed aspirational displays. Many similar shots to nature-centric perfume commercials can be seen in “Perfume,” several of which are displayed below:



Figure: Collage from the “Perfume” music video


These familiar conventions provided “Perfume” with an established framework to adapt, allowing it to convey a “refreshing” image– a valued trait within the K-Pop industry. Themes of aspirational luxury also play a role in “Perfume,” yet with a slightly different approach from Western perfume commercials. The music video was entirely recorded in Thailand, a choice in setting departing from the typical European destinations. Many of the scenes take place inside a rustic-style indoor dome, which aligns more with a “nature” centric image; however, other scenes take place outside a luxurious, European-style mansion, which comes complete with a swimming pool that the members dive into– showcasing themes of released inhibitions, freedom, and youth. Furthermore, NCT DOJAEJUNG, the protagonists of the “Perfume” music video, are all Asian, breaking in tradition with the typically white actor seen in perfume commercials. However, the music video also (briefly) features a female model, who is the receiver of the perfumes designed by the members. This model, shown in the collage above, is white, visually matching the common protagonist of a Western perfume commercial. The combination of these choices help distinguish “Perfume” as a product within an Asian industry, while also keeping it in alignment with a Western industry it sets out to emulate.
Western fragrance commercials frequently exhibit a gendered set of conventions, both towards men and women. Within male-marketed commercials, an attractive male model or celebrity tends to be featured, who is often shirtless or in minimal clothing that reveals a chiseled, muscular form. Other commercials showcase men wearing outfits commonly deemed as “masculine,” such as black leather jackets, white dress shirts, and navy suits. The contents of many of these commercials can be categorized into two categories: those showing the men participating in strenuous physical activities, giving the cologne an association with masculine power and strength; and those showing the men being desired by women and engaging in sexuality with them, giving the cologne an association with desirability and sexual dominance. Male-marketed fragrance commercials also typically refrain from floral imagery and the use of bright colors, especially shades of purple and pink.
“Perfume”, however, subverts many of these conventions, framing its male subjects in a “softer” manner more typical of a female-marketed perfume commercial. None of the members display muscular forms or wear any of the aforementioned typically “masculine” outfits; only member Doyoung dons a cotton white dress shirt, and certain shots of him wearing it reveal his collarbone in a manner more consistent with scenes of female models. Other styling choices in “Perfume” that seemingly oppose the “masculinity” portrayed in cologne commercials include the members’ use of necklaces and earrings; the usage of vivid colors, such as orange and purple; the multilayered, free-flowing outfit combinations; and the choice of certain fabrics, such as silk, mesh, and sequin. Aligning with the previously-mentioned “natural” aspirational image, which is associated with “femininity,” the video also heavily features floral imagery—the members carry flowers, dance in rooms surrounded by them, run while surrounded by falling petals, and even appear to be crafting perfumes derived from flowers, to name a few examples.
This portrayal, while differing from male-marketed fragrance marketing, aligns more with male performances of gender typically seen throughout the K-Pop industry. A common male image in the industry is the “Flower Boy,” a concept that “frames male idols with an effeminate appearance, typically adorned with makeup, jewelry, unisex clothing, and displays of vulnerability and liminal masculinity.” (qtd. in Bao and Wijitsopon 2025, pg. 52) Even when men are featured at the center, in the K-Pop industry, the primary audience usually consists of women, making adapting influences from another industry to evolving female tastes a key component to success. This shift reflects how K-pop reshapes masculine imagery to appeal to a predominantly female audience, softening the dominance-driven masculinity typical of Western fragrance advertising.
While Western perfume commercials frequently make use of models, they also commonly feature celebrities, using the pre-existing associations surrounding that celebrity as a marketing tactic. In her paper “ Communicative Anosmia and Poetics of Storytelling in Perfume Commercials,” Halszka Leleń writes that perfume commercials “evidently capitalize on the multiple, polyphonic intertextual associations activated in addressee’s minds by seeing their favorite actresses or actors starring in a given film.” Within Perfume, these associations are capitalized on even further, as not only does each group member have his own distinct set of associated qualities, but strong, devoted parasocial relationships between fans and idols are the norm within the K-Pop industry. All three members are brand ambassadors for high-end fashion brands, with Jaehyun collaborating with Prada in 2022, Doyoung with Dolce & Gabbana in 2023, and Jungwoo with Tods’ in 2023 (Loh, et. al, 2026). These partnerships give the members an image of luxury, adding to the intended effect of the Perfume album’s promotional materials and music video. Parasocial relationships also play an important role in Perfume’s creative and marketing strategies. Before re-debuting as a trio, the members of NCT DOJAEJUNG had spent several years as members of NCT 127, establishing on-screen dynamics as individuals, with each other, and with their fans. Fans already felt like they “knew” the members, giving the opportunity to create an even stronger response to seeing them “star” in film-like materials that portrayed them in romantic scenarios. With this method, Perfume extends the logic of celebrity endorsement beyond recognition into sustained emotional attachment, a key distinction from traditional perfume advertising.

title: Perfume
section: Fostering Parasocial Connections Through Multisensory Experiences
text:
Fostering Parasocial Connections Through Multisensory Experiences
Beyond visual and celebrity-driven strategies, Perfume further deepens its engagement through multisensory and narrative techniques. Throughout the “Perfume” music video, a loose narrative centers around each of the members designing perfumes and collectively branding themselves as “Proust House,” twice delivering these perfumes to the doorstep of the female model briefly featured throughout the video. Aligning with this reference to French author Marcel Proust, whose novel In Search of Lost Time notably connected scent and taste to emotional memories, many of the lyrics throughout the Perfume album heavily draw upon this connection. Beginning with the first verse of “Perfume,” the titular track, which opens by immediately introducing fragrance-themed lines (“I’ll fill your day with heavenly scents / To remind you of my name at every step”), a significant amount of the song’s lyrics consist of metaphors linking together the qualities of perfume and emotional connections, particularly focusing on feelings of sensuality. The song’s chorus, in particular, (“Leaving ‘em on your wrist / On your collar / Gonna let ‘em hover / So you can’t forget me / Mixed in with the smallest sigh”) centers around making these connections. The lyrics first highlight the wrist and collar, two “pulse points” of the body where scent is marketed by many perfume companies to last the longest (“Pulse Points and Beyond” 2025), in order to evoke the physical experience of applying perfume and tie in a layer of intimacy to it. Even the song’s dance choreography emphasizes this act, having the members deftly raise their wrists to their noses as they sing the associated lyrics. The preceding lyric then adds a layer of emotional significance to this physical experience by tying memories of the song’s speaker to the fragrance being applied, making sure that the applicant of the perfume “can’t forget” him. The final lyric further drives in this dual connection with a phrase open for interpretation, as a “smallest sigh” could emerge from a sense of satisfaction derived from a fragrance’s scent, or it could be a subtle expression of lovesickness.
This sensory strategy extends beyond scent alone. “Strawberry Sunday,” another song on Perfume, lyrically associates another sense– taste– to memories and feelings of intimacy. The song first recalls memories of a cherished night spent together, with the chorus going on to describe it as a “perfect Strawberry Sunday.” This association between a close memory and a sweet dessert (a strawberry sundae) brings together two seemingly unrelated sensory experiences, using the favorable feelings listeners likely have towards desserts to create and strengthen positive feelings with the singers. As the song progresses, the song clearly lays out this connection, with lyrics stating, “This emotion we feel is breakfast / A lovely kiss to open up both our days,” “It’s always new, at long last / Time for dessert,” “You taste so sweet,” “White clouds like ice cream,” and “Sunday, at the start and end of every week / I’m cravin’ you / Deeply melting honeydew.” Taste has become a growing aspect of perfume conceptualization, with a trend of “gourmand” (edible-scenting) fragrances carrying over into both Western and Korean perfume markets. “Strawberry Sunday” capitalizes on this trend, further expanding upon the album’s concept of sensory-based memory activation.
Another sensory-related aspect of the Perfume album was the creation of a perfume unique to each member. In a YouTube video titled “THE BLOTTER PAPER: Blending Process,” released on April 2, 2023, the group’s members describe the creative process behind the scent selection and naming of their fragrances. One member, Doyoung, describes his rationale, recounting his love for the “transition from winter to spring” and his desire to capture that phenomenon in a scent (NCT, 0:25-40). He also connects his scent choices to the role he plays in the group, stating, “I was thinking, ‘What would I look like as part of DOJAEJUNG?’…I want to be the most subtle part of this team.” (NCT, 1:31-2:06) These statements tie back to the practices of storytelling and memory-evocative scent within the Korean perfume industry, showcasing NCT DOJAEJUNG’s adept connection to this market. Among the multiple versions of the physical copy of the album, three of the versions shipped with a blotter paper containing the scents that the members described in this video (one version for each individual member’s fragrance). Fans now had the opportunity to experience each scent, creating new associations in between their perceptions of the members and these thoughtfully crafted scents– further heightening their parasocial connections.
These songs’ lyrics and the conception of the physical scent cards may be intentional attempts to evoke the “Proust Phenomenon,” which is an “ immediate and involuntary recall (‘cued recall’) through an odour-invoked memory.” (Groes and Mercer 2021) Creating strong associations between scent (in the form of perfume) and emotional memories is not only the goal of this album, but the goal of the perfume industry as a whole, putting NCT DOJAEJUNG’s Perfume in alignment with the industry whose aesthetics and marketing tactics it set out to emulate and adapt to the K-pop market.

title: Perfume
text:
Conclusion
Going back to Lee Soo-man’s proposed concept of “cultural technology,” the intersection in Perfume of two highly aesthetically-based mediums that carry across cultural implications (perfume commercials and K-Pop albums) adds up more, both creatively and financially. Adapting Western influences with a Korean lens aids in spreading Korean culture across the globe while also appealing towards regional preferences, a key goal of the “cultural technology” system. As K-Pop continues to expand globally, more potential opportunities lie in crafting creative concepts fusing together global and domestic industries. Several other K-Pop concepts, even within NCT, have taken direct influences from outside industries– the designer car aesthetic used throughout 2 Baddies by NCT 127, the hockey player concept in the music video for “90s Love” by NCT U, and the martial arts movie aesthetic in the “Kick It” music video by NCT 127, to name a few examples. However, fewer examples exist that fully commit to adapting an external inspiration in the thorough way that Perfume does, which will make any future attempts interesting to observe.
